+ PCL CONSTRUCTION ENTERPRISES, INC. [3 Press Releases]

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION BREAKS GROUND ON $190 MILLION CONTRACT FOR STATE-OF-THE-ART DRINKING WATER FACILITY

DENVER, Colo. (Sept. 25, 2025) - PCL Construction, Inc. celebrated the groundbreaking of a $190 million contract to build the Westminster Boulevard Drinking Water Facility - an innovative project that employs advanced treatment technology to ensure clean water to residents.

The City of Westminster entrusted the PCL team to build the project's most critical components. As the lead contractor, PCL will manage the construction of cutting-edge filtration and treatment technologies. The facility will feature advanced oxidation and filtration systems designed to improve taste and odor, while enhancing resiliency against fluctuations in incoming water quality.

Designed with long-term sustainability in mind, the project replaces aging infrastructure and will provide reliable drinking water to nearly 33,000 homes and businesses. Once operational the facility will have the capacity to treat 14.7 million gallons of water per day (MGD).

"PCL is proud to celebrate the opening of the Westminster Boulevard Drinking Water Facility with the City of Westminster. This is an essential project that secures safe, reliable drinking water for the community," said Ankur Talwar, PCL's West region district manager.

The groundbreaking ceremony brought together Westminster City Council members, build partners, local leaders, and community members to celebrate the future site.

The facility is slated to be finished in 2028.

Source: Company Website

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION AND PHOENIX CHILDREN'S: BUILDING A STRONGER FUTURE FOR PEDIATRIC CARE

PHOENIX, Ariz. (November 10, 2025) - PCL Construction, Inc. and Phoenix Children's are proud to celebrate a partnership dedicated to advancing pediatric health and supporting children facing critical medical challenges in Arizona.

For eight consecutive years, PCL Construction in Arizona has deepened its commitment to Phoenix Children's, increasing its annual donation to $50,000 in support of pediatric cancer care. This ongoing collaboration directly benefits children by funding medical specialists, cutting-edge technology, and family-centered care.

"Our partnership with Phoenix Children's is about more than sponsorship-it's about building hope and supporting families during their most difficult times," said Josh Yates, construction risk manager at PCL Construction. "We are honored to contribute to the hospital's mission and to help create brighter futures for children in our community."

As part of this partnership, PCL proudly sponsors Phoenix Children's annual Big Dig for Kids event. This unique experience gives pediatric patients a chance to explore construction equipment, participate in hands-on activities, and enjoy a day filled with fun and inspiration. The event not only raises vital funds for pediatric cancer care but also brings moments of joy to children and families, helping them envision a future beyond illness.

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION ENTERPRISES, INC. DAVID CAMPBELL NAMED TO AZ BIG MEDIA'S 2026 "PEOPLE TO KNOW IN COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE" LIST

PHOENIX, Arizona (November 6, 2025) - PCL Buildings Inc. is pleased to announce that David Campbell, district manager for PCL Construction's Phoenix buildings office, has been named to AZ Big Media's "2026 People and Projects to Know in Commercial Real Estate" list. This annual recognition celebrates influential leaders who are shaping the future of Arizona's commercial real estate industry.

"This recognition highlights the momentum we're building in Arizona's construction market and our commitment to contributing positively to the region's future," says Campbell. "I'm proud to represent PCL and support the growth and innovation happening across Arizona."

Campbell was named as the leader of PCL's newest commercial buildings office in Phoenix this past spring, building upon a nearly 40-year legacy of excellence in water and civil construction across the Southwest. The Phoenix Buildings office marks a strategic milestone in PCL's commitment to the region, offering construction expertise across sectors including hospitality, manufacturing, aviation, and commercial and retail development.

His appearance on AZ Big Media's list reflects Campbell's contributions to the industry over the course of his nearly 20-year construction career, having led a wide range of high-profile projects across the U.S. Campbell joined PCL in 2005, with early contributions to entertainment and hospitality developments in Orlando. He later served as senior project manager on the American Dream megamall in New Jersey and held several leadership roles at PCL's U.S. head office in Denver before his current role in Phoenix.

+ MCCARTHY BUILDING COMPANIES, INC. RYAN HOUSE DEBUTS TEEN SUITES FOR KIDS LIVING LONGER WITH LIFE-LIMITING CONDITIONS

Ryan House, a nonprofit that provides respite and end-of-life care to children with life-limiting conditions, recently capped off its 15th anniversary year by unveiling two newly renovated teen suites. The renovations allow Ryan House to better serve children who are now surviving well into adolescence and young adulthood and were made possible through generous support from McCarthy Building Companies, which selected Ryan House as a recipient of the company's Heart Hats(TradeMark) grants, and ARC Document Solutions.

Thanks to advances in diagnostics, disease management, and comprehensive care, many children with life-limiting conditions are now living longer than once expected. Ryan House is evolving alongside the children and families it serves, making meaningful changes to better support teens through environments designed specifically for this stage of life.

The renovation was spearheaded by McCarthy's Specialized Solutions Group, which focuses on complex renovations and technical builds within healthcare and other specialized environments. The donation involved the renovation of two private suites specifically designed for teenagers and features superhero-themed decor provided by ARC that fully immerses them in a comic book-inspired atmosphere. The rooms also now include fully adjustable desks to accommodate different mobility needs, allowing teens to comfortably do schoolwork or creative activities. Each suite is equipped with the latest state-of-the-art gaming system and adaptive controllers, plus customizable LED lighting that can change colors based on each teen's preferences. Additionally, bathroom updates were made to allow for enhanced comfort and accessibility.

"This renovation speaks to how pediatric care has changed over the past decade and a half," said Dr. Wendy Bernatavicius, medical director at Ryan House and division chief of primary, complex care and adolescent medicine at Phoenix Children's Hospital. "We're seeing more adolescents and young adults with complex needs living vibrant lives. Improved medical care, early intervention and whole-family support have extended the lifespan of many of these teens. That means rethinking the environments where they're cared for. These teen suites are not just rooms. They're an affirmation of identity, belonging, and respect for each young person who calls Ryan House their home away from home."

During their recent holiday break, Ryan House welcomed two familiar guests, brothers Mason and Jaxton, who are living with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, as the first teens to stay in the newly renovated suites. While they've stayed at Ryan House before, this visit marked the first time they experienced the upgraded rooms designed specifically with teens, like them, in mind.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP, LLC GREEN FROM THE INSIDE OUT: BERKELEY LAB SETS NEW STANDARDS

Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory's Biological and Environmental Program Integration Center sets new sustainability standards. Berkeley Lab Biological and Environmental Program Integration Center (BioEPIC) unites scientists from LBNL's biosciences and earth and environmental sciences areas in a four-story, 71,000-square-foot facility that sets a new standard for sustainability. Completed in March, the facility offers a suite of next-generation tools designed to study how microbial communities interact with plants and soil to influence the environment.

Inside, 200 researchers perform carefully controlled experiments on microbe-soil-plant interactions with instruments and computing infrastructure that connect BioEPIC to its field research sites. These unique climate research environments, along with three additional levels of labs and collaborative spaces, are housed in an all-electric facility designed to maximize energy efficiency and resiliency.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP, LLC FROM CONCEPT TO REALITY: HARNESSING 3D PRINTING AT CLARK'S MID-ATLANTIC YARD

Clark Construction's Mid-Atlantic Yard in Upper Marlboro, Maryland, is a powerhouse of specialized services, from fleet oversight to custom fabrication. This diverse facility is also part of a transformative initiative: the integration of 3D printing for prototyping innovative custom solutions to solve complex challenges.

The method came to life when faced with a challenge: designing a custom cleaning piece for a drill rig auger. While the traditional route would involve costly and time-consuming steel fabrication for a prototype, Charles Harman III, a craft and machine shop supervisor, and his team explored a new solution for this type of fabrication and successfully used a 3D printer to create a scaled model. The successful prototype demonstrated the efficacy of the system, and once the 3D printed model was approved, the team began assembling the steel-fabricated version.

urn:contentItem:6JGB-XRV3-RRRH-604K-00000-00SWINERTON INCORPORATED - HIGHLIGHTS FROM AUSTIN INDUSTRIES, INC. AND PEER COMPANIES2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:38:29ZLexisNexisSTOCKSWINERTON INCORPORATED - HIGHLIGHTS FROM AUSTIN INDUSTRIES, INC. AND PEER COMPANIES

DASHBOARD: SWINERTON INCORPORATED

PermID4296594671Websitehttp://www.swinerton.comIndustryConstruction & EngineeringAddress5707 SOUTHWEST PARKWAY, BLDG. 1, SUITE 200 | AUSTIN, TX 78735

ACTIVITIES:

Swinerton Incorporated provides commercial construction and construction management services in the Western United States. It offers general construction, modernization and renovation, management and consulting, preconstruction, corporate, and renewable energy services. The companys projects include aviation, cultural, education, federal, gaming and entertainment, healthcare, hospitality, interiors, mission critical, mixed-use/residential, office, renewable energy, retail, and special. Swinerton Incorporated was formerly known as Swinerton & Walberg Co. The company was founded in 1888 and is based in San Francisco, California with additional offices in California, Colorado, Hawaii, Texas, New Mexico, and Washington.

+ SWINERTON INCORPORATED [3 Press Releases]

+ SWINERTON TAPS PROVEN DUO TO DRIVE NATIONAL ACCOUNTS

With over $1 billion in growth and over 250 major projects under their belts, Swinerton's Chris Chany and Derek Jaschke step into new roles to drive client-focused expansion. Swinerton announces two strategic leadership promotions to continue delivering exceptional client experiences nationwide. Effective July 1, Chris Chany has been promoted to lead National Markets, while Derek Jaschke has been named Director of National Accounts, succeeding Chany.

Chany, known for his relationship-driven leadership style, previously served as Director of National Accounts, where he was instrumental in expanding Swinerton's client base and driving growth. Under his leadership, the national accounts sales model grew from $350 million to more than $1 billion in just four years. Chany has strengthened partnerships across multiple markets and sectors, generating repeat business.

"Chris' ability to strategize with teams from pre-sell to pursuit, through project execution and closeout, has allowed Swinerton to grow and solidify critical client relationships and seize growth opportunities for our teams," shares Don Adair, Swinerton's CRO. "Throughout his career, Chris has emphasized relationships, extraordinary client experiences, dependable project delivery and consistently generating repeat business."

Source: Company Website

+ SWINERTON INCORPORATED WILLIAM SILVA NAMED DIRECTOR OF NATIONAL MASS TIMBER

Instrumental leader to enhance Swinerton Builders' mass timber capabilities Swinerton, a national commercial general contractor and construction manager with office locations nationwide, continues its commitment to advancing and accelerating the adoption of mass timber construction with the appointment of William Silva as Director, National Mass Timber.

In this position, Silva will lead the creation of a Mass Timber Center of Excellence, a cross-functional initiative designed by Swinerton to drive innovation, collaboration and integrate the company's extensive general contracting expertise with its affiliate firm, Timberlab. Timberlab's specialized capabilities include mass timber procurement, manufacturing, fabrication, engineering and design. The center will serve as a hub for innovation, education, and operational excellence, empowering Swinerton teams nationwide to deliver exceptional mass timber projects and continue to be a trusted resource for its clients, design partners, and engineering partners.

Source: Company Website

+ SWINERTON TOPS-OUT LATEST MASS TIMBER MULTIFAMILY PROJECT IN CHARLOTTE

Swinerton, an industry-leading commercial general contractor with offices nationwide, announces the topping out of Cordo, an $80 million multifamily development in Charlotte's Optimist Park and the construction firm's fifth collaboration with Space Craft. Just blocks from The Joinery and Oxbow, also developed by Space Craft and built by Swinerton, Cordo is designed to be a walkable, transit-friendly community for active families and young professionals. The hybrid-structure broke ground last fall and is expected to be ready for occupancy by summer 2027.

"There is a distinct comfort level when you have a long-term relationship with your construction crew that spans multiple projects," says Brian Nicholson, director of construction, Space Craft. "Cordo is the fifth project we've embarked on with Swinerton, and the team continues to impress us with an unwavering commitment to quality and ability to deliver on our vision. With each community, we set out to build a sustainable, modern neighborhood for today. Swinerton has been instrumental in helping us shape the Mill District into one of Charlotte's hottest neighborhoods."

Situated on a three-acre site in Charlotte's Optimist Park community, Cordo totals seven floors with 3,000 square feet of street-level retail just steps from Cordelia Park, Optimist Hall and LYNX Blue Line light rail access. The 325,000-square foot development features 287studio, one-, two-, three- and four-bedroom units and offers premium amenities including a rooftop terrace, private courtyard, electric bicycles for rent, a fitness center, a shared dining area and coworking and meeting space. A Mass timber brings a biophilic element into each residence with cross-laminated timber (CLT) decks creating exposed timber ceilings. Charlotte architecture firm Shook Kelley designed the community located at 515 Parkwood Ave.

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ AUSTIN INDUSTRIES, INC. [3 Press Releases]

+ AUSTIN NAMED NO. 1 SCIENCE + TECHNOLOGY CONSTRUCTION FIRM

DALLAS - Austin is honored to once again be named the No. 1 Science + Technology Facility Construction Firm in the U.S. by World of Construction.

‍

From semiconductor fabs to digital infrastructure, our teams pour their skill, care, and dedication into creating the specialized spaces that help fuel tomorrow's breakthroughs. It's thanks to our employee‑owners, partners, and clients that this work is possible, and we're grateful for the trust they place in us every day.

‍

Firms recognized by World of Construction are those that are leading the way in delivering some of the most advanced science and technology facilities in the world. Austin is grateful for the recognition and proud to be leading our industry.

Source: Company Website

+ AUSTIN EARNS MULTIPLE HONORS AT ASA HOUSTON EVENT

HOUSTON - Austin Commercial was honored to be finalists for multiple Excellence in Construction Awards from American Subcontractors Association-Houston Chapter. Austin's Houston office was awarded the GC Safety Award and the Project of the Year Over $50M for the UH Memorial Hermann Football Operations Center.

‍

The company extends its thanks to our trade partners and employee-owners for all that you do, and for making these honors possible.

Source: Company Website

+ AUSTIN RANKED #27 ON BD+C'S TOP 100 HOSPITAL FACILITY CONSTRUCTION FIRMS

DALLAS - Austin has been named No. 27 on Building Design + Construction's (BD+C) 2025 Top 100 Hospital Facility Construction Firms list, reaffirming the company's long-standing position as a trusted builder in the healthcare market, and highlighting continued growth in this sector.

Austin has appeared on BD+C's healthcare rankings for years, but recent performance reflects a notable expansion in both volume and opportunity. That momentum is already visible in the work underway. In 2025, Austin began preconstruction on several major healthcare projects, including JPS Health Network Hospital Addition, The University of Texas at Austin & The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center Medical Complex Project (MCP), and The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center Patient Care Building 1 (1PC).

This ranking is a testament to the expertise and dedication of Austin's employee-owners and highlights the importance of continued investment in people and resources as healthcare remains a key growth sector for the company.

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ QUANTA SERVICES, INC. [1 Press Release]

+ QUANTA SERVICES TO HOST & WEBCAST ITS 2026 INVESTOR DAY

Quanta Services, Inc. (NYSE: PWR) announced that it will host and webcast its 2026 Investor Day from New York City on Tues., Mar. 31, 2026. Management intends to present certain information and engage in a question-and-answer discussion focused on the company's strategic initiatives and new long-term financial targets. The management presentation portion of the event is scheduled to begin at approximately 9:00 a.m. Eastern time and the entire event, including the question-and-answer portion, is expected to conclude by approximately 11:30 a.m. Eastern time.

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ BARTON MALOW COMPANY [4 Press Releases]

+ BARTON MALOW + SHERIDAN CONSTRUCTION SET TO BUILD NEW MACON ARENA

MACON, GEORGIA - Barton Malow, in association with Sheridan Construction, has been chosen as the Construction Manager for the new Macon Arena, a major project that will replace the current Macon Coliseum and serve as a key part of the region's expanding entertainment opportunities.

Located southeast of Atlanta in Macon-Bibb County, the planned facility is designed to host various multipurpose events like minor league hockey, basketball games, high school tournaments, concerts, conferences, trade shows, and more. The new arena will offer a top-tier guest experience, featuring premium seating options like luxury suites, club suites, and loge boxes, as well as modern fan amenities such as clubs, concessions, restrooms, locker rooms, and integrated technologies. Back-of-house and support spaces will ensure the venue functions as a state-of-the-art civic facility, offering performers, event planners, and organizations a great experience.

"This arena won't be just a venue, it will be a destination and major economic engine that creates opportunities, attracts visitors, and keeps our city competitive for years to come," said Macon-Bibb Mayor Lester Miller.

As Macon's entertainment landscape continues to expand with the new Atrium Health Amphitheater and the recently renovated Macon City Auditorium, the new arena is poised to further elevate the region's growing lineup of elite venues.

"We're thrilled to partner with Sheridan Construction and bring Barton Malow's expertise to Macon-Bibb to deliver a top-tier arena. The new venue is set to become a central location in Georgia for sports, entertainment, and community gatherings," said Barton Malow Office Director Blake Boyd. "This project is an investment in Macon-Bibb County's future, and we're excited to support the local community every step of the way."

Adds Sheridan Construction President + CEO Christy Kovac. "It's a privilege for our firm to return to Macon's legacy of entertainment and civic pride, having built the original coliseum 57 years ago. Partnering with Barton Malow on this transformative new arena is both an honor and a responsibility. We believe this project will serve as a powerful economic and tourism engine, attracting visitors, creating opportunities, and enriching the lives of everyone in our community for generations to come."

Construction is planned to commence in summer 2026, with the project anticipated to reach completion in 2028.

Source: Company Website

+ BARTON MALOW COMPANY LUKE KNOCHEL PROMOTED TO VICE PRESIDENT OF PROJECT CONTROLS

Luke's project controls leadership role spans multiple geographic regions and consists of overseeing directors and managers across Barton Malow's operations.

Barton Malow has announced the promotion of Luke Knochel to Vice President of Project Controls.

In his new role, Luke leads a multi‑disciplinary team responsible for core functions such as cost and schedule management, forecasting, project start‑up, accounts receivable and payable processes, and change management. His leadership spans multiple geographic regions, overseeing directors and managers across Barton Malow's operations.

Luke started at Barton Malow in 2013 as a Cost Analyst and has served in various project controls and finance leadership roles throughout his tenure. He oversaw accounts payable and accounts receivable, change management, forecasting, reporting, and more on the Little Caesars Arena project, then went on to supervise Barton Malow's offices in Chicago, Columbus, and Nashville. After a promotion to Project Controls Manager overseeing the then-Midwest business unit, Luke transitioned into a corporate finance role managing the shared service department and implementing new accounts payable software.

Barton Malow has been awarded the EcoVadis Bronze Medal, placing the construction enterprise among the top 35% of companies worldwide for sustainability performance. Today, Matt sees things from the other side of the table. After leaving Embry-Riddle in 2022, he spent two years as Executive Director of NIL Collective Operations + Development at Athlete Forward Initiative. + TURNER CONSTRUCTION U.S. CONSTRUCTION DEMAND REMAINS STRONG AMID SHIFTING MARKET CONDITIONS

Turner Construction Company announced that the First Quarter 2026 Turner Building Cost Index-which measures costs in the non-residential building construction market in the United States-increased to the value of 1530. This represents a 1.32% quarterly increase from the Fourth Quarter 2025 and a 4.87% yearly increase from the First Quarter 2025.

"Demand across the U.S. construction market remains solid, though conditions vary by region and sector. High-growth areas such as data centers, manufacturing, and semiconductor facilities are driving strong demand, particularly in the Midwest and Southeast, while traditional commercial construction has softened in several markets. At the same time, architecture and engineering activity appears to be stabilizing. Material costs such as steel, aluminum, and copper have experienced increases during the first quarter," said Attilio Rivetti, the Turner vice president responsible for compiling the Cost Index. "We are closely monitoring rising oil prices, which could begin to impact transportation and material costs in the coming quarter."

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION ENTERPRISES, INC. [3 Press Releases]

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION BREAKS GROUND ON $190 MILLION CONTRACT FOR STATE-OF-THE-ART DRINKING WATER FACILITY

DENVER, Colo. (Sept. 25, 2025) - PCL Construction, Inc. celebrated the groundbreaking of a $190 million contract to build the Westminster Boulevard Drinking Water Facility - an innovative project that employs advanced treatment technology to ensure clean water to residents.

The City of Westminster entrusted the PCL team to build the project's most critical components. As the lead contractor, PCL will manage the construction of cutting-edge filtration and treatment technologies. The facility will feature advanced oxidation and filtration systems designed to improve taste and odor, while enhancing resiliency against fluctuations in incoming water quality.

Designed with long-term sustainability in mind, the project replaces aging infrastructure and will provide reliable drinking water to nearly 33,000 homes and businesses. Once operational the facility will have the capacity to treat 14.7 million gallons of water per day (MGD).

"PCL is proud to celebrate the opening of the Westminster Boulevard Drinking Water Facility with the City of Westminster. This is an essential project that secures safe, reliable drinking water for the community," said Ankur Talwar, PCL's West region district manager.

The groundbreaking ceremony brought together Westminster City Council members, build partners, local leaders, and community members to celebrate the future site.

The facility is slated to be finished in 2028.

Source: Company Website

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION AND PHOENIX CHILDREN'S: BUILDING A STRONGER FUTURE FOR PEDIATRIC CARE

PHOENIX, Ariz. (November 10, 2025) - PCL Construction, Inc. and Phoenix Children's are proud to celebrate a partnership dedicated to advancing pediatric health and supporting children facing critical medical challenges in Arizona.

For eight consecutive years, PCL Construction in Arizona has deepened its commitment to Phoenix Children's, increasing its annual donation to $50,000 in support of pediatric cancer care. This ongoing collaboration directly benefits children by funding medical specialists, cutting-edge technology, and family-centered care.

"Our partnership with Phoenix Children's is about more than sponsorship-it's about building hope and supporting families during their most difficult times," said Josh Yates, construction risk manager at PCL Construction. "We are honored to contribute to the hospital's mission and to help create brighter futures for children in our community."

As part of this partnership, PCL proudly sponsors Phoenix Children's annual Big Dig for Kids event. This unique experience gives pediatric patients a chance to explore construction equipment, participate in hands-on activities, and enjoy a day filled with fun and inspiration. The event not only raises vital funds for pediatric cancer care but also brings moments of joy to children and families, helping them envision a future beyond illness.

+ PCL CONSTRUCTION ENTERPRISES, INC. DAVID CAMPBELL NAMED TO AZ BIG MEDIA'S 2026 "PEOPLE TO KNOW IN COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE" LIST

PHOENIX, Arizona (November 6, 2025) - PCL Buildings Inc. is pleased to announce that David Campbell, district manager for PCL Construction's Phoenix buildings office, has been named to AZ Big Media's "2026 People and Projects to Know in Commercial Real Estate" list. This annual recognition celebrates influential leaders who are shaping the future of Arizona's commercial real estate industry.

"This recognition highlights the momentum we're building in Arizona's construction market and our commitment to contributing positively to the region's future," says Campbell. "I'm proud to represent PCL and support the growth and innovation happening across Arizona."

Campbell was named as the leader of PCL's newest commercial buildings office in Phoenix this past spring, building upon a nearly 40-year legacy of excellence in water and civil construction across the Southwest. The Phoenix Buildings office marks a strategic milestone in PCL's commitment to the region, offering construction expertise across sectors including hospitality, manufacturing, aviation, and commercial and retail development.

His appearance on AZ Big Media's list reflects Campbell's contributions to the industry over the course of his nearly 20-year construction career, having led a wide range of high-profile projects across the U.S. Campbell joined PCL in 2005, with early contributions to entertainment and hospitality developments in Orlando. He later served as senior project manager on the American Dream megamall in New Jersey and held several leadership roles at PCL's U.S. head office in Denver before his current role in Phoenix.

+ MCCARTHY BUILDING COMPANIES, INC. RYAN HOUSE DEBUTS TEEN SUITES FOR KIDS LIVING LONGER WITH LIFE-LIMITING CONDITIONS

Ryan House, a nonprofit that provides respite and end-of-life care to children with life-limiting conditions, recently capped off its 15th anniversary year by unveiling two newly renovated teen suites. The renovations allow Ryan House to better serve children who are now surviving well into adolescence and young adulthood and were made possible through generous support from McCarthy Building Companies, which selected Ryan House as a recipient of the company's Heart Hats(TradeMark) grants, and ARC Document Solutions.

Thanks to advances in diagnostics, disease management, and comprehensive care, many children with life-limiting conditions are now living longer than once expected. Ryan House is evolving alongside the children and families it serves, making meaningful changes to better support teens through environments designed specifically for this stage of life.

The renovation was spearheaded by McCarthy's Specialized Solutions Group, which focuses on complex renovations and technical builds within healthcare and other specialized environments. The donation involved the renovation of two private suites specifically designed for teenagers and features superhero-themed decor provided by ARC that fully immerses them in a comic book-inspired atmosphere. The rooms also now include fully adjustable desks to accommodate different mobility needs, allowing teens to comfortably do schoolwork or creative activities. Each suite is equipped with the latest state-of-the-art gaming system and adaptive controllers, plus customizable LED lighting that can change colors based on each teen's preferences. Additionally, bathroom updates were made to allow for enhanced comfort and accessibility.

"This renovation speaks to how pediatric care has changed over the past decade and a half," said Dr. Wendy Bernatavicius, medical director at Ryan House and division chief of primary, complex care and adolescent medicine at Phoenix Children's Hospital. "We're seeing more adolescents and young adults with complex needs living vibrant lives. Improved medical care, early intervention and whole-family support have extended the lifespan of many of these teens. That means rethinking the environments where they're cared for. These teen suites are not just rooms. They're an affirmation of identity, belonging, and respect for each young person who calls Ryan House their home away from home."

During their recent holiday break, Ryan House welcomed two familiar guests, brothers Mason and Jaxton, who are living with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, as the first teens to stay in the newly renovated suites. While they've stayed at Ryan House before, this visit marked the first time they experienced the upgraded rooms designed specifically with teens, like them, in mind.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP, LLC GREEN FROM THE INSIDE OUT: BERKELEY LAB SETS NEW STANDARDS

Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory's Biological and Environmental Program Integration Center sets new sustainability standards. Berkeley Lab Biological and Environmental Program Integration Center (BioEPIC) unites scientists from LBNL's biosciences and earth and environmental sciences areas in a four-story, 71,000-square-foot facility that sets a new standard for sustainability. Completed in March, the facility offers a suite of next-generation tools designed to study how microbial communities interact with plants and soil to influence the environment.

Inside, 200 researchers perform carefully controlled experiments on microbe-soil-plant interactions with instruments and computing infrastructure that connect BioEPIC to its field research sites. These unique climate research environments, along with three additional levels of labs and collaborative spaces, are housed in an all-electric facility designed to maximize energy efficiency and resiliency.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP, LLC FROM CONCEPT TO REALITY: HARNESSING 3D PRINTING AT CLARK'S MID-ATLANTIC YARD

Clark Construction's Mid-Atlantic Yard in Upper Marlboro, Maryland, is a powerhouse of specialized services, from fleet oversight to custom fabrication. This diverse facility is also part of a transformative initiative: the integration of 3D printing for prototyping innovative custom solutions to solve complex challenges.

The method came to life when faced with a challenge: designing a custom cleaning piece for a drill rig auger. While the traditional route would involve costly and time-consuming steel fabrication for a prototype, Charles Harman III, a craft and machine shop supervisor, and his team explored a new solution for this type of fabrication and successfully used a 3D printer to create a scaled model. The successful prototype demonstrated the efficacy of the system, and once the 3D printed model was approved, the team began assembling the steel-fabricated version.

urn:contentItem:6JK9-6SS3-RTN1-V2DN-00000-00'Why not us?'2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisCONVERSATIONS'Why not us?'Eric Monson

Joe Zemba

La Junta Tribune-Democrat

Could Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties ebcome Colorado's next renewable energy boom belt?

From the eastern plains of Colorado, the skyline is changing in places most people still think of as "empty."

In Kit Carson County, what used to be uninterrupted grassland and farmland now looks like a slow-moving industrial forest of steel and blades. More than 300 wind turbines are already spinning or rising there, with billions of dollars in new projects still in the pipeline.

The question quietly echoing across neighboring counties like Otero, Bent County, and Crowley County is simple but loaded: If it's working there… why not us?

Kit Carson County was not an obvious energy hub. It wasn't a metro, it wasn't a tech corridor, and it wasn't chasing developers.

But it had three things that changed everything: Open, flat land with consistent wind; willing landowners; and a critical transmission line corridor cutting through the region.

That last piece, Xcel Energy's transmission "backbone" project, turned the county into what one developer called a "field of dreams for energy." Once the line was there, companies followed.

Now, major developers like NextEra Energy Resources and Apex Energy are investing billions, building wind farms with names like Dusty Rose and Singing Grass, while hundreds of construction workers move in and out of towns smaller than many high school graduating classes.

The economic impact is already measurable. Millions in annual property tax revenue, long-term lease payments to landowners, and new industrial tax base in one of Colorado's poorest rural regions.

So why hasn't the same boom happened in Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties?

The answer is not simple, but it's also not one single barrier.

Wind and solar don't succeed on wind alone; they succeed where power can be moved.

Kit Carson County benefited from proximity to high-voltage infrastructure feeding the Front Range. In contrast, parts of the Arkansas Valley still lack the same level of transmission capacity upgrades needed to support utility-scale expansion.

Without that "energy highway," developers often hesitate.

Next, there is policy uncertainty and competition.

Colorado has aggressive renewable energy goals, but development is competitive.

Developers tend to cluster where permitting is predictable, land assembly is simple, and infrastructure already exists.

Kit Carson became a first mover. That momentum attracts more momentum.

Lastly, local appetite matters.

Kit Carson County commissioners took a relatively neutral "property rights first" stance: if landowners want to lease, the county generally allows it.

In other counties, conversations around industrial-scale energy projects have been more cautious; balancing agriculture identity, scenic concerns, and long-term land use questions.

That caution doesn't block development, but it can slow it.

So, could it work here?

Short answer: yesbut not automatically.

The Arkansas Valley has several natural advantages:

Strong wind corridors across open plains, large agricultural land parcels suitable for turbine spacing, proximity to existing regional transmission infrastructure in parts of the grid, and a workforce already familiar with agriculture-heavy land management.

Energy analysts often note that southeastern Colorado is technically viable for wind expansion.

But viability is not the same as investment readiness.

For Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties to see a Kit Carson–style boom, several conditions would likely need to align:

Transmission expansion

New or upgraded lines would be the single biggest catalyst. Without them, projects remain theoretical.

County-level planning clarity

Clear zoning and permitting rules reduce risk for developers and landowners alike.

Landowner participation

Kit Carson's growth was largely driven by private land leasesnot top-down planning.

Utility demand signals

Developers build where buyers are lined uputilities like Xcel Energy or regional cooperatives signaling long-term purchase agreements.

If the Tri-County region does attract large-scale renewable development, the impact could mirror what Kit Carson is now experiencing:

Property tax growth

Wind farms are already becoming major tax contributors in Kit Carson County, rivaling agriculture. For rural counties with tight budgets, that shift can mean new funding for roads, schools, and emergency services.

Construction jobs and local spending

Each project brings hundreds of temporary construction jobs and millions in local economic activity during build-out phases.

Long-term lease payments provide steady revenue, but that can supplement agricultural uncertainty.

Counties can evolve from purely agricultural economies into hybrid agricultural–energy regions.

But growth also comes with questions that Kit Carson itself continues to wrestle with: Permanent changes to rural landscapes, decommissioning and long-term turbine removal responsibility, wildlife and habitat considerations, and community division over land use priorities.

Even supporters acknowledge: once the towers go up, the skyline changes for generations.

So… why not us? The most honest answer may be this: It is not a question of wind. It is a question of timing, infrastructure, and political will.

Kit Carson County did not set out to become a renewable energy hub, it became one because the conditions aligned, and because landowners, developers, and utilities all moved in the same direction at the same time.

For Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties, the opportunity is not hypothetical. It is already sitting on the same wind-swept plains.

The real question now is whether those counties will position themselves to catch the next wave or watch it pass by on the horizon.

This article includes reporting assistance from generative AI.

urn:contentItem:6JK1-STW3-RSH2-M2P0-00000-00Top Workplaces 2026: The best large companies to work for in Colorado2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:54ZLexisNexisBUSINESSTop Workplaces 2026: The best large companies to work for in ColoradoSpecial to The Denver Post

Editor's note: This information was taken from the Energage database. The businesses provide the data, and it may have changed since it was first entered into the database.

No. 1

Luminate Bank

Years named: 1

Founded: 2021

Headquarters: Minneapolis/St.Paul, Minnesota

Employees: 754, with 56 in Colorado

Facts: Luminate Bank provides nationwide banking, specialized business services, and comprehensive mortgage lending. Formerly known as Equity Bank, it focuses on digital-forward banking, offering high-yield savings, CDs, and tailored commercial/title industry services.

No. 2

Elevations Credit Union

Years named: 4

Founded: 1952

Headquarters: Boulder

Employees: 544

Locations: 19

Facts: Elevations is an award-winning Colorado financial institution with over $3 billion in assets and over 170,000 members.

No. 3

LONG Building Technologies

Years named: 12

Founded: 1965

Headquarters: Littleton

Employees: 626, with 338 in Colorado

Locations: 5 in Colorado

Facts: LONG Building utilizes building technology services to automate, enhance efficiency, make facilities greener, and ensure safety for commercial, institutional, and industrial facilities of all types and sizes.

No. 4

Simpson Housing

Years named: 3, 2 consecutive

Founded: 1948

Headquarters: Denver

Employees: 629, with 133 in Colorado

Facts: Simpson Housing is an experienced, fully integrated real estate firm that provides services in commercial and multifamily property management, development, and construction.

No. 5

Enverus

Years named: 2

Founded: 1999

Headquarters: Austin, Texas

Employees: 1,596, with 103 in Colorado

Facts: Enverus is a SaaS company that uses generative AI to deliver analytics and insights, providing benchmark cost and revenue data from over 95% of U.S. energy producers. It enhances production, distribution, and renewable energy development, serving over 6,000 customers in 50 countries.

No. 6

ENSCO

Years named: 3, 2 consecutive

Founded: 1969

Headquarters: Vienna, Virginia

Employees: 761, with 155 in Colorado

Locations: 2 in Colorado

Facts: ENSCO, Inc. is a technology company with $180 million in revenue. It provides engineering and technology solutions in aerospace, national security, and surface transportation, with a focus on safety and reliability.

No. 7

Solari Crisis & Human Services

Years named: 1

Founded: 2007

Headquarters: Tempe, Arizona

Employees: 796, with 153 in Colorado

Facts: Solari Crisis & Human Services operates 24/7 crisis hotlines and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, and offers behavioral health services. Known formerly as Crisis Response Network, it specializes in rapid crisis call response, mobile crisis team dispatch, and 911 diversion services in Arizona, Oklahoma, and Colorado.

No. 8

Pinnacol Assurance

Years named: 10

Founded: 1915

Headquarters: Denver

Employees: 633

Facts: Pinnacol Assurance provides workers' compensation protection to more than 50,000 Colorado employers and their employees.

No. 9

Stanley Consultants

Years named: 2, 1 consecutive

Founded: 1913

Headquarters: Muscatine, Iowa

Employees: 900, with 152 in Colorado

Facts: Stanley Consultants is a global, employee-owned firm providing engineering, environmental, and construction services

No. 10

Edward Jones

Years named: 14

Founded: 1922

Headquarters: St. Louis, Mo.

Employees: 55,000, with 959 in Colorado

Locations: 365 in Colorado

Facts: Edward Jones has nearly 20,000 financial advisors who work directly with 9 million clients and $2.2 trillion in client assets.

No. 11

Catholic Charities of Denver

Years named: 2

Founded: 1927

Headquarters: Denver

Employees: 550

Facts: Catholic Charities, Archdiocese of Denver, provides services to 50,000 people through 30 programs in northern Colorado. It focuses on defending human dignity and caring for children, families, and those in need.

No. 12

Breckenridge Grand Vacations

Years named: 12, 7 consecutive

Founded: 1985

Headquarters: Breckenridge

Employees: 700

Locations: 7 in Colorado

Facts: Breckenridge Grand Vacations is a family-owned company that builds, sells, and manages fractional resorts in Breckenridge.

No. 13

Flexential

Years named: 5, 1 consecutive

Founded: 1999

Headquarters: Centennial

Employees: 930

Locations: 5 in Colorado

Facts: Flexential is a leading provider of hybrid IT infrastructure services, offering colocation, cloud, connectivity, and managed solutions across a nationwide data center footprint.

No. 14

Thompson Thrift

Years named: 3

Founded: 1986

Headquarters: Indianapolis

Employees: 675, with 71 in Colorado

Facts: Thompson Thrift is a full-service real estate company that develops multifamily, mixed-use, retail, industrial, and commercial projects across the nation.

No. 15

AllHealth Network

Years named: 8

Founded: 1955

Headquarters: Englewood

Employees: 900, with 650 in Colorado

Locations: 14 in Colorado

Facts: AllHealth Network provides behavioral health care to more than 20,000 children, adults, older adults, families, and couples. Its services include counseling, psychiatry, crisis services, substance abuse treatment, an adult acute treatment unit, services offered to the criminal justice system, vocational and social rehabilitation, school-based services, and an on-site pharmacy.

No. 16

Ping Identity Corp

Years named: 10, 1 consecutive

Founded: 2002

Headquarters: Englewood

Employees: 1,975, with 339 in Colorado

Facts: Ping Identity is an enterprise-grade identity and access management (IAM) software company that provides secure, intelligent authentication for employees, partners, and customers.

No. 17

Coalfire

Years named: 7, 1 consecutive

Founded: 2001

Headquarters: Westminster

Employees: 1,000, with 100 in Colorado

Facts: Coalfire is a premier cybersecurity advisory and assessment firm established in 2001, specializing in cloud security, risk management, and compliance for technology, healthcare, and financial industries.

No. 18

Waterton

Years named: 1

Founded: 1995

Headquarters: Chicago

Employees: 930, with 53 in Colorado

Facts: Waterton is a real estate investment and property management company with a focus on U.S. multifamily and hospitality properties.

No. 19

Colorado State University Global

Years named: 1

Founded: 2007

Headquarters: Denver

Employees: 715

Facts: CSU Global is a public, not-for-profit, 100% online state university. It is a legally independent, third campus of the Colorado State University System, which also includes CSU in Fort Collins and CSU Pueblo

No. 20

Loenbro

Years named: 13, 1 consecutive

Founded: 2000

Headquarters: Westminster

Employees: 4,000, with 927 in Colorado

Facts: Loenbro is an industrial construction and specialized services provider offering comprehensive solutions, including industrial construction, electrical, mechanical, fabrication, and inspection services for industries such as energy, mining, and manufacturing.

No. 21

Vectra Bank Colorado

Years named: 5

Founded: 1998

Headquarters: Denver

Employees: 530

Locations: 35

Facts: Vectra serves Colorado's small and middle-market businesses and their owners, managers, and employees through full-service branches.

No. 22

GOAL Academy High School

Years named: 4

Founded: 2009

Headquarters: Pueblo

Employees: 680

Facts: GOAL High School delivers innovative blended online learning experiences and provides cutting-edge technology to more than 6,000 students across 40 locations statewide.

No. 23

Constant Contact

Years named: 1

Founded: 1995

Headquarters: Waltham, Massachusetts

Employees: 990, with 143 in Colorado

Facts: Constant Contact is a comprehensive digital marketing platform designed primarily for small businesses and nonprofits to create, send, and track email marketing campaigns.

No. 24

Brilliant Earth

Years named: 1

Founded: 2005

Headquarters: San Francisco

Employees: 725, with 61 in Colorado

Facts: Brilliant Earth is a publicly traded jewelry company that specializes in ethically sourced, high-quality engagement rings, wedding bands, and fine jewelry.

value.Title: 400MWh Weld Energy Storage Project in Platte River , Colorado Advances as PRPA Finishes Key Steps

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-35H3-S02V-B1P3-00000-00400MWh Weld Energy Storage Project in Platte River, Colorado Advances as PRPA Finishes Key Steps2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:38:28ZLexisNexis400MWh Weld Energy Storage Project in Platte River, Colorado Advances as PRPA Finishes Key Steps

Platte River Power Authority (PRPA) is kicking off construction of the 400MWh Weld Energy Storage project in Colorado. The project is the first for the wholesale power provider for Fort Collins and three other communities. Furthermore, the project will be the utilitys first utility-scale battery project. It is part of its efforts to go as far as it believes it can to rely on all-non carbon energy sources.

Construction is set to commence in May and a groundbreaking ceremony set to happen on June 4. This is according to information presented during the April 30 board meeting. Once complete, the system will be able to store 400 megawatt hours of power.

Moreover, it will allow PRPA to charge the battery when renewable energy is abundant and prices are low and use it later. NextEra Energy Resources will build and own the project, which will be located at the Severance substation, right next to the Black Hollow Sun solar project. Solar and battery storage projects are becoming fundamental with states making more investments to advance renewable energy.

For instance, The Eland Solar-plus-Storage Center in Los Angeles, California developed by Arevon became operational 10 months ago. With 758MW of solar photovoltaic capacity and a 1,200MWh battery energy storage system, the $2 billion mega-project represents a major leap toward LAs clean energy goals.

Other Energy Projects by PRPA other than Weld Energy Storage Project in Colorado

Other than the Weld Energy Storage project in Colorado, PRPA is focused on construction of five aeroderivative gas turbines. The utility says the turbines are needed to provide dispatchable capacity that can be fired up when renewable sources like wind and solar are not meeting the demand. However, the move has drawn criticism from environmental advocates.

They note that the $623 million investment could become a stranded asset that ratepayers will have to fund even if they are cleaner alternatives available after it is built. However, PRPA notes the technology is actually what enables it to pursue renewables more aggressively. It notes it is because it provides a way to fill the gaps when renewables are not meeting demand. The state construction permit, another step in the process to complete the project, was issued April 15.

The state utility has already been granted a 1041 permit from Larimer County. The turbines are expected to be up and running before the scheduled closure of the Rawhide 1 coal unit in December 2029. However, there will be a 180-day period where the coal plant will be allowed to operate at the same time as the new turbines while they are being tested. Once the turbines are cleared for commercial dispatch, Rawhide 1 will close.

Project Factsheet

Project Name: Weld Energy Storage Project

Location: Platte River region, Colorado, USA (Severance Substation)

Estimated Investment Value: Not disclosed

Project Type: Utility-scale battery energy storage system

Timeline

April 2026: Key permits secured and approvals completed

May 2026: Construction commencement

June 4, 2026: Groundbreaking ceremony scheduled

Completion: Not officially disclosed

Site & Scale

Storage Capacity: 400 MWh

Project Type: Battery energy storage system (BESS)

Location Detail: Adjacent to Black Hollow solar project

Primary Use: Store excess renewable energy and dispatch when needed

Project Teams

Utility / Offtaker: Platte River Power Authority (PRPA)

Developer / Owner / Builder: NextEra Energy Resources

Infrastructure Scope

400MWh battery storage installation

Integration with nearby solar generation (Black Hollow Solar Project)

Connection to Severance substation

Grid support systems for load balancing and dispatch

Strategic Objectives

Support transition to non-carbon energy sources

Improve grid reliability and flexibility

Store excess renewable energy during low demand periods

Reduce reliance on fossil fuel peaking generation

Enable higher penetration of wind and solar energy

Challenges

Intermittency of renewable energy sources

Dependence on complementary systems like gas turbines

Criticism over parallel investment in gas infrastructure

Uncertain long-term cost dynamics of storage vs alternatives

Current Status

State construction permit issued (April 15, 2026)

1041 permit granted by Larimer County

Construction set to begin with groundbreaking in June

Marks PRPAs first utility-scale battery storage project

value.Jurisdiction: Colorado
value.Title: 'Why not us?'

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-8G63-S46F-Y0Y7-00000-00'Why not us?'2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisNEWS'Why not us?'T'Naus Nieto

Joe Zemba

La Junta Tribune-Democrat

Could Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties ebcome Colorado's next renewable energy boom belt?

From the eastern plains of Colorado, the skyline is changing in places most people still think of as "empty."

In Kit Carson County, what used to be uninterrupted grassland and farmland now looks like a slow-moving industrial forest of steel and blades. More than 300 wind turbines are already spinning or rising there, with billions of dollars in new projects still in the pipeline.

The question quietly echoing across neighboring counties like Otero, Bent County, and Crowley County is simple but loaded: If it's working there… why not us?

Kit Carson County was not an obvious energy hub. It wasn't a metro, it wasn't a tech corridor, and it wasn't chasing developers.

But it had three things that changed everything: Open, flat land with consistent wind; willing landowners; and a critical transmission line corridor cutting through the region.

That last piece, Xcel Energy's transmission "backbone" project, turned the county into what one developer called a "field of dreams for energy." Once the line was there, companies followed.

Now, major developers like NextEra Energy Resources and Apex Energy are investing billions, building wind farms with names like Dusty Rose and Singing Grass, while hundreds of construction workers move in and out of towns smaller than many high school graduating classes.

The economic impact is already measurable. Millions in annual property tax revenue, long-term lease payments to landowners, and new industrial tax base in one of Colorado's poorest rural regions.

So why hasn't the same boom happened in Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties?

The answer is not simple, but it's also not one single barrier.

Wind and solar don't succeed on wind alone; they succeed where power can be moved.

Kit Carson County benefited from proximity to high-voltage infrastructure feeding the Front Range. In contrast, parts of the Arkansas Valley still lack the same level of transmission capacity upgrades needed to support utility-scale expansion.

Without that "energy highway," developers often hesitate.

Next, there is policy uncertainty and competition.

Colorado has aggressive renewable energy goals, but development is competitive.

Developers tend to cluster where permitting is predictable, land assembly is simple, and infrastructure already exists.

Kit Carson became a first mover. That momentum attracts more momentum.

Lastly, local appetite matters.

Kit Carson County commissioners took a relatively neutral "property rights first" stance: if landowners want to lease, the county generally allows it.

In other counties, conversations around industrial-scale energy projects have been more cautious; balancing agriculture identity, scenic concerns, and long-term land use questions.

That caution doesn't block development, but it can slow it.

So, could it work here?

Short answer: yesbut not automatically.

The Arkansas Valley has several natural advantages:

Strong wind corridors across open plains, large agricultural land parcels suitable for turbine spacing, proximity to existing regional transmission infrastructure in parts of the grid, and a workforce already familiar with agriculture-heavy land management.

Energy analysts often note that southeastern Colorado is technically viable for wind expansion.

But viability is not the same as investment readiness.

For Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties to see a Kit Carson–style boom, several conditions would likely need to align:

Transmission expansion

New or upgraded lines would be the single biggest catalyst. Without them, projects remain theoretical.

County-level planning clarity

Clear zoning and permitting rules reduce risk for developers and landowners alike.

Landowner participation

Kit Carson's growth was largely driven by private land leasesnot top-down planning.

Utility demand signals

Developers build where buyers are lined uputilities like Xcel Energy or regional cooperatives signaling long-term purchase agreements.

If the Tri-County region does attract large-scale renewable development, the impact could mirror what Kit Carson is now experiencing:

Property tax growth

Wind farms are already becoming major tax contributors in Kit Carson County, rivaling agriculture. For rural counties with tight budgets, that shift can mean new funding for roads, schools, and emergency services.

Construction jobs and local spending

Each project brings hundreds of temporary construction jobs and millions in local economic activity during build-out phases.

Long-term lease payments provide steady revenue, but that can supplement agricultural uncertainty.

Counties can evolve from purely agricultural economies into hybrid agricultural–energy regions.

But growth also comes with questions that Kit Carson itself continues to wrestle with: Permanent changes to rural landscapes, decommissioning and long-term turbine removal responsibility, wildlife and habitat considerations, and community division over land use priorities.

Even supporters acknowledge: once the towers go up, the skyline changes for generations.

So… why not us? The most honest answer may be this: It is not a question of wind. It is a question of timing, infrastructure, and political will.

Kit Carson County did not set out to become a renewable energy hub, it became one because the conditions aligned, and because landowners, developers, and utilities all moved in the same direction at the same time.

For Otero, Bent, and Crowley counties, the opportunity is not hypothetical. It is already sitting on the same wind-swept plains.

The real question now is whether those counties will position themselves to catch the next wave or watch it pass by on the horizon.

This article includes reporting assistance from generative AI.

value.Title: BILL TO MODERNIZE THE PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, PROTECT RATEPAYERS AND IMPROVE OVERSIGHT CLEARS COMMITTEE

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-8WH3-S75N-T0NV-00000-00BILL TO MODERNIZE THE PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, PROTECT RATEPAYERS AND IMPROVE OVERSIGHT CLEARS COMMITTEE2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisBILL TO MODERNIZE THE PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, PROTECT RATEPAYERS AND IMPROVE OVERSIGHT CLEARS COMMITTEEStates News ServiceDENVER, CO

The following information was released by the Colorado Senate Democrats:

Written By Senate Democrats

The Senate Finance Committee today passed the Public Utilities Commission (PUC) Sunset. This legislation would extend the critical functions of the PUC while modernizing the commission to meet the needs of Coloradans.

HB26-1326 is sponsored by Senate Majority Leader Robert Rodriguez, D-Denver, and Assistant Majority Leader Lisa Cutter, D-Jefferson County. Without the bill, the PUC would expire on September 1, 2026.

"This bill ensures Colorado continues to lead in renewable energy and consumer protection, while prioritizing safety in our transportation, communications, and utility systems," said Rodriguez. "We're extending and modernizing the PUC to reflect today's realities and set us up for the future."

"How we travel, communicate, and power our lives all look completely different today than they did when the PUC was last renewed seven years ago," said Cutter. "After months of work and negotiations between impacted groups, this bill strikes a balance that boosts renewable energy, strengthens safety from passenger rail to rideshare trips, cracks down on phone scams and bad actors, and improves community collaboration."

The PUC is the primary regulator of Colorado's electric, gas, water, telecommunications and transportation services. In2019, the PUC Sunset established a minimum value for the cost of carbon pollution. This helped modernize benefits to ratepayers and improve Colorado's clean energy transition.

HB26-1326 would extend the PUC's critical functions for another seven years while modernizing and boosting transparency within the agency. This would continue Colorado's clean energy transition that will lower utility costs and foster new jobs.

Meeting Colorado's renewable energy goals

To help Colorado meet its energy goals, this bill would update and streamline clean energy reporting requirements and scheduling for utility companies. The bill would boost transparency and accountability by allowing the PUC to investigate how to streamline and integrate energy planning proceedings and report its findings to the General Assembly. The bill would also help electrical utilities secure more renewable energy assets, such as wind and solar, by requiring the PUC to conduct a study regarding the barriers companies face towards joint procurement, or collaborative purchasing for a large-scale investment.

Improving Safety

This bill takes steps to improve rail, pipeline and transportation safety and security in Colorado. Under HB26-1326, state rail oversight would be aligned with federal law for consistency. The bill also includes the creation of an oversight program that would review, approve and monitor the creation and implementation of passenger and freight rail in Colorado.

The bill would also require rideshare companies to provide the commission's contact information to riders for increased transparency. PUC staff receiving complaints would receive trauma informed training. HB26-1326 also requires activity buses, limos, and off-road scenic charters to receive scheduled inspections by the commission to ensure they are safe for travel.

Modernizing telecommunications and protecting consumers

Mobile, wireless, cellular, landline and satellite telecommunications fall under the PUC's purview and are charged a fee to provide service in Colorado to help maintain and expand our state's telecommunications infrastructure. This bill extends the fee to include more telecommunications systems, including web-based service providers, such as Google Voice or Zoom Phone.

To boost consumer protections and crack down on bad actors, this bill would increase the fees for companies that purchase no-call lists and sell them to other companies.

Improving local participation and engagement

HB26-1326 would encourage more local participation and decision-making by requiring the PUC to hire staff dedicated to engagement and communications to ensure inclusiveness and consistency in public comment hearings. To further improve representation, the PUC would create an equity task force to represent the interests of disproportionately impacted communities, workers, and income-qualified customers. The PUC would also be required to conduct a study on income-based energy assistance programs to improve funding access and equity.

The bill now heads to the Senate Appropriations Committee for further consideration.

value.Jurisdiction: Colorado
value.Title: House Bill 1030 Let’s bring data centers to state on our terms

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ0-2XK3-SGCK-H0NT-00000-00House Bill 10302026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 13House Bill 1030Let’s bring data centers to state on our termsBill Owens ; Guest Commentary

Data centers could provide the real economic investment we need in our current budget crisis.

As someone who has had the privilege of leading the state of Colorado, I take our economic competitiveness and leadership very seriously. I fought to ensure our state was open for business a place where entrepreneurs would want to locate, bringing jobs and investment with them. We are also a state with a long tradition of balancing growth with stewardship.

Over the decades, Colorado has experienced extraordinary growth and faced transitions that reshaped its economy and infrastructure. We are a hub for countless major industries from aerospace and energy to healthcare and manufacturing, driven by a talented workforce, smart policies and our own pioneering spirit. Oil and natural gas development and the growth of clean power each brought opportunity alongside challenges.

The question is not whether change will come but whether Colorado would help shape it on its own terms or react to decisions made elsewhere. Experience taught us that the best outcomes and lasting opportunities come neither from rushing ahead without proper protections in place nor from standing still but through thoughtful and collaborative planning. This is the opportunity I believe we have with House Bill 1030 the chance to lead again.

To remain in our position at the forefront of innovation, we should take steps toward powering our future by bringing data center investment and jobs to the Centennial State. House Bill 1030 will do that by providing a targeted state (not local) sales tax exemption to attract data center development to communities hoping to benefit, but it ensures that we bring them here on our terms.

Data centers operate behind the scenes as the digital engines that power our daily lives and support the key industries that fuel Colorado’s economy. Many states raced to attract data centers, hoping to benefit from the thousands of jobs, critical infrastructure and economic growth they bring with them. However, some states did fail to put guardrails in place.

But in Colorado we can learn from these early mistakes and protect consumers and our environment. We can then leverage data center development to create thousands of jobs, strengthen our grid and infrastructure, boost our renewable energy industry, support the state’s thriving tech sector and provide needed economic investment and tax revenue for communities devastated by the loss of power generation, mines, plants and oil and gas.

Data centers support employment throughout their entire life cycle, from initial construction to daily operations and ongoing community involvement. Developing these facilities relies on a wide range of skilled workers including electricians, engineers, technicians and other trades. Just six new data centers could bring 1,500 construction jobs over a period of up to three years, with average annual wages exceeding $140,000 per worker. Each center also typically supports up to 100 high-paying, permanent roles. These are good jobs and real money, especially in our rural communities that are full of skilled workers just looking for an opportunity.

Data centers also make major investments to our infrastructure by funding improvements to roads, utilities, broadband networks and water systems that benefit entire regions. They play a key role in strengthening the power grid by financing new transmission upgrades and advanced energy technologies that increase reliability and capacity for everyone.

It’s no secret that data centers use a lot of energy, but House Bill 1030 provides strong, enforceable ratepayer protections that ensure rates don’t go up and data centers pay their own way. This includes making large and critical infrastructure upgrades to our aging power grid. Data centers also can power our clean-energy economy. Colorado is a national leader in clean energy and is uniquely positioned to create data-center policies that help us reach our ambitious energy goals.

Finally, although data centers are the digital backbone for Colorado’s thriving tech industry on the Front Range, they also can offer a lifeline for communities on the Western Slope and Eastern Plains that have suffered from the loss of power generation and other industries. They bring stable jobs, modern infrastructure, community investments and long-term tax revenue that funds schools, public safety and other essential services.

There is a reason other states are working aggressively to attract data centers and it’s to the tune of billions. Far from “corporate welfare,” the incentives provided to bring data centers to Colorado will be dwarfed by what they bring in in real wages, property taxes, infrastructure investments and economic growth. Nationally data centers generated $162.7 billion in revenue for communities. Colorado currently captures only 2% of that investment and actually has lost data center jobs.

Other states moved too quickly, and Colorado can and should learn from their experiences. However, we have a history of crafting the strategic, forward-thinking policies that prove that economic strength and environmental responsibility can advance together and that other states will follow.

That same approach is needed now. The policy choices we make around data centers today will influence Colorado’s economy, workforce and infrastructure for years to come, and we don’t want to be left behind. Our state has never shied away from hard conversations about growth, resources and responsibility. Colorado can shape the digital future if we choose to lead with House Bill 1030.

Bill Owens is a former governor of Colorado.

value.Jurisdiction: Colorado; New Mexico
value.Title: Recent power outages expose the cost permitting delays (guest opinion)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHJ-0NG3-S3X2-84W2-00000-00Recent power outages expose the cost permitting delays (guest opinion)2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisNEWSRecent power outages expose the cost permitting delays (guest opinion)Guest Opinion

By Melissa Simpson, Lynn Granger and Chelsie Miera

Colorado residents have been plagued by major power outages every month since December. The outages have come during severe weather, when it's extremely dry and the winds are high. When the lights go out, the consequences are immediate. Food spoils, personal medical devices stop working and families are left scrambling.

These blackouts are more than inconvenient. They expose real vulnerabilities in our electric grid and raise an urgent question: What are Colorado's leaders doing to address the problems?

One recent event left 120,000 customers without power across NorthernColorado, the Front Range, and the foothill communities. Some residents were without power for four days. This spring, even as temperatures warm, public utilities continue warning residents about the risk of outages across major portions of the state.

Colorado's outages are local events, but the challenge they point to is national. Across the country, extreme weather is stressing the grid, aging infrastructure is becoming harder to ignore, and the demand for reliable electricity keeps rising.

So how did we get here, and what is the fix?

Part of the answer is that building and modernizing energy infrastructure takes far too long. Permits must move through layers of review, environmental analysis can drag on for years, and lawsuits can add even more delay. The result is a system that often makes it harder, not easier, to build the transmission lines, power plants and supporting infrastructure we need to keep the grid reliable.

According to experts at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, only one in five interstate electrical transmission projects planned between 2000 and 2017 had begun operating by the end of 2022. Stanford University reports that nearly one in three major energy and infrastructure projects faced lawsuits before construction could begin.

In our region, the TransWest Express transmission line took roughly 16 years to move from permitting to construction. In New Mexico, SunZia took 17 years to build. Both connected renewable energy projects to the grid.

We experience similar problems in oil and natural gas development, which matters for reliability and power generation. Natural gas generates 43% of electricity nationwide and 34% in Colorado. Yet projects that could support that supply often face the same slow-moving approval process and the same legal roadblocks.

On the West Slope, the Bull Mountain Unit was proposed in 2008 and did not receive the green light to proceed until last June. Years of environmental review and legal challenges held it up. That kind of delay is not just frustrating for industry. It also delays the infrastructure and energy supply communities depend on.

This is where Colorado's congressional delegation has an opportunity to lead.

In the U.S. House, Rep. Gabe Evans is helping advance bipartisan efforts to streamline permitting for electricity, pipeline, and transmission projects. The House also passed the SPEED Act, which would modernize the National Environmental Policy Act so federal agencies can complete permitting more efficiently while still maintaining environmental safeguards.

In the Senate, where progress has been slower, any serious permitting reform will move through the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee where Sen. John Hickenlooper serves. He would have a chance to help break the partisan stalemate and deliver a process that works better for both energy development and environmental protection.

The point is not to weaken safeguards or ignore legitimate concerns. It is to make sure that projects needed for reliability, affordability and public safety are not trapped for years in a process that too often delays the obvious and prolongs the preventable.

Leadership at this moment means focusing less on blame and more on solutions. It means strengthening the grid so families are less likely to experience outages. And it means building infrastructure in a way that is timely, responsible, and durable.

When the power goes out, safety and health matter more than partisanship. Colorado's leaders in Congress should act accordingly.

Melissa Simpson is president of Western Energy Alliance. Lynn Granger is president and CEO of Colorado Oil & Gas Association. Chelsie Miera is executive director of West Slope Colorado Oil & Gas Association.

value.Title: Can the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-0VR3-S169-R4KR-00000-00Can the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisCan the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?
